subject.title: Mathematics Analysis and Approaches (AA)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics Analysis and Approaches** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **number and algebra, functions, geometry and trigonometry, statistics and probability, and calculus**, with a strong focus on **theoretical understanding, formal mathematical reasoning, and analytical problem-solving**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Non-calculator written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 2** | Calculator allowed written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 3** | Calculator allowed problem-solving | N/A | 60 minutes | 20% (HL only) |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal Assessment | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Clear logical sequence with introduction, methodology, data presentation, analysis and conclusion
Well-structured with headings, sub-headings, numbered tables and figures
weaknesses: Verbose narrative and repeated caveats add bulk without advancing the mathematics
Inconsistent bibliography formatting and missing DOI/URLs
Conclusion repeats calculations verbatim rather than synthesizing results
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments place the work in band 3 for coherence and organization but only band 2 for conciseness. The report is generally well organized and coherent, justifying 3/4, despite verbosity and reference inconsistencies that prevent the top band.

strengths: Effective use of multiple representations (tables, scatter-plot, annotated graphs)
Logical flow signposted by clear Interpretation heading
weaknesses: Inconsistent and sometimes incorrect notation (HTML tags in RÂ², mismatched variable names)
Missing axis labels and units in figures, unclear summation in chi-square formula
Vague explanations of some steps and handling of missing data
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Mixed strand scores (2 for notation, 3 for representations, 2 for clarity) average to band 2. Notation and formatting flaws alongside gaps in justification limit this to 2/4.

strengths: Demonstrates independent thinking by designing and administering a personal survey
Clear personal approach with commentary on Gen Z usage
Evidence of creativity in combining regression, calculus and chi-square
weaknesses: Applications sometimes feel routine or forced (e.g. integrating a straight line)
Creativity and initiative good but not highly innovative
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments (1, 2, 2) average to about 1.7, indicating clear evidence of personal engagement but not outstanding. 2/3 reflects significant but not exceptional engagement.

strengths: Meaningful reflection on limitations such as small sample size
Thoughtful comparison of results and acknowledgement of caveats
Clear evaluation with concrete improvement suggestions
weaknesses: Lacks deep critical analysis of approximation errors or p-value significance
Some terminology errors in discussing chi-square outcomes
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Consistent band-2 assessments across all strands indicate meaningful but not substantial reflection. A 2/3 appropriately reflects thoughtful but not deep or comprehensive analysis.

strengths: Relevant use of regression, Pearson r, definite integral and chi-square in context
Hypotheses clearly stated, demonstrating appropriate statistical setup
Techniques are commensurate with SL course level
weaknesses: Partial understanding: integral never evaluated, mis-counted degrees of freedom, inconsistent R metrics
Units misuse and lack of function definition for integral limits
Significant calculation and rounding errors in chi-square table
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments (3, 3, 2) average about 2.7. The student applies appropriate SL-level mathematics but multiple significant errors prevent band 4 or above. A 3/6 reflects competent yet flawed application.

strandSelections.reasoning: Explanations of each calculation are provided in order, but some steps are vague (e.g. simply stating Google Sheets delivered the regression) and logical justification for the chi-square set-up is thin. Overall clarity is mixed â acceptable but not consistently strong.

The student shows partial understanding: regression slope is used correctly, but the integral of a straight line is not evaluated; the chi-square degrees of freedom are mis-counted; units for area are meaningless. Errors are significant but not pervasive, matching âsome understanding with errorsâ.

The student supplies raw and processed tables, a scatter-plot with a trend-line, and an annotated version of the same graph. These multiple representations are appropriate and clearly linked to the discussion, showing effective (though not exemplary) use.

The report follows a clear sequence â introduction, methodology, data presentation, calculations, analysis, conclusion â and each section builds on the previous one. Although some arguments (e.g. the simultaneous use of correlation and a chi-square test) are not fully reconciled, the line of thought is generally easy to follow from start to finish, demonstrating overall coherence and logical development.

While the student attempts to mix calculus, regression, and chi-square, these applications are fairly routine and sometimes forced (e.g. integrating a straight line). Creativity and initiative are evident but limited.

Results from each technique are interpreted and compared; the contradiction between correlation and chi-square is discussed briefly. This constitutes thoughtful but not âdeep and insightfulâ analysis.

The conclusion summarises findings, re-states caveats and proposes concrete improvements (larger sample, additional variables). This is a clear evaluation of outcomes with some implications, aligning with band 2.

The student reflects on what the area under the curve âcould representâ and acknowledges limitations such as small sample size and missing variables, providing meaningful â though not deep â reflection throughout.

Standard symbols (â«, R, ÏÂ²) are used, but formatting is inconsistent (e.g. html tags in RÂ², mismatched variables, units written inside the integral result). Some notation mistakes persist, showing only partial control of mathematical language.
The topic arises from the studentâs own curiosity, and personal comments about âGen Zâ usage and future improvements indicate a clear personal approach, albeit not highly innovative.

Lengthy narrative paragraphs defining everyday terms and repeated caveats about âcorrelation not causationâ add bulk without advancing the mathematics. Nevertheless most material still relates to the aim, and outright irrelevancies are limited. This fits âmostly relevant with some unnecessary elementsâ.

Linear regression, Pearson r, definite integral, and chi-square are all mathematically relevant to the stated aim and are used in context. Although the integral is unnecessary, it is still an appropriate mathematical tool, so relevance is judged âappropriateâ.
Designing and administering a personal survey and selecting several different statistical tools demonstrates significant independent thinking, even though the mathematical depth is moderate.

Headings, sub-headings, numbered tables and labelled figures give the exploration a recognisable structure with introduction, body and conclusion. Cross-referencing (e.g. âtable 1â, âfigure 1â) is mostly correct. A few abrupt jumps (such as inserting calculus before any justification) keep it from the highest standard but the organization is clearly above âadequateâ.
All techniques employed are at or above SL level (basic calculus, correlation, ÏÂ²). Their sophistication is moderate, but clearly commensurate with course expectations, fulfilling band 3.

annotations.comment: Acknowledging small sample sizes for certain screen-time categories demonstrates critical thinking about data reliability.
The bibliography entries lack a consistent citation style (e.g. APA or MLA). Standardize formatting for author names, titles, and dates.
The Plant and Soil Sciences citation omits a proper URL or DOI. Complete this reference to meet academic standards.
The Methodology section header clearly delineates the research design, aiding readers in locating key procedural information.
The Interpretation heading on page 9 effectively signals a transition to data discussion, enhancing the reportâs logical flow.

The introduction provides context but contains lengthy narrative on everyday behaviors that could be condensed. Focus more on mathematical aims rather than general descriptions to improve conciseness and relevance.
The âDefining Termsâ section is verbose and repeats motivational discussion. Trim this to definitions that directly support the mathematical exploration.
The integral notation is introduced without explicitly defining the screen-time function f(t). Specify how f(t) is derived from data and justify the limits a and b.
Discussing calculus before justifying its relevance appears forced. Clarify why integral calculus provides unique insight compared to simpler summaries like totals or means.
The repeated caveat âcorrelation does not imply causationâ while accurate, adds bulk without advancing analysis. Mention once and then focus on mathematical results.
Methodology description lacks detail on sampling, survey distribution and potential biases. Include how participants were selected to strengthen validity.
Raw data table has many empty cells. Explain how missing data were handled or consider eliminating incomplete entries to avoid skewed analysis.
In Table 2 the variable names x and y are switched in the caption compared to previous sections. Maintain consistent notation to avoid confusion.
Figure 1 lacks clear axis labels and units. Label both axes with variable names and units (hours, percentage) to improve interpretation.
Describing the area under the graph as a trapezoid without justifying linearity oversimplifies the data. Discuss approximation error if data deviate from perfect straight line.
The inline use of html tags in RÂ² (e.g. <sup> tags) indicates inconsistent notation. Use LaTeX style: $$R^2$$ throughout.
The definite integral setup is correct but never evaluated. Either carry out the integration or justify why area via trapezoid is preferred.
The trapezoid area formula uses only end values 93 and 73 but ignores the intercept at x=3 and x=10. Re-examine the correct y-values for each boundary.
Labeling the gradient as $$m=-3.32\%\text{ per hour}$$ conflates units. Present the gradient in consistent units, e.g. percentage-points per hour.
The Pearsonâs $$R=-0.9$$ here conflicts with the earlier stated $$R^2=0.936$$. Clarify which metric is reported and ensure consistency.
The hypotheses are clearly stated, demonstrating appropriate statistical setup for chi-square testing.
Contingency table categories differ from earlier binned data (missing rows 7â10). Maintain consistent groupings across analyses.
The expected-frequency formula is correct but cell values seem over-rounded. Ensure at least two decimal places for small expected counts.
The chi-square formula listing lacks a sigma summation sign and context for summing all cells. Add $$\chi^2=\sum_{i}\frac{(O_i-E_i)^2}{E_i}$$ notation.
The table of lengthy chi-square terms correctly computes 18.25 but df calculation uses 8 columns instead of 4, giving df=21 incorrectly.
The analysis section begins well but lacks deeper discussion of approximation errors or validity of linear assumptions. Develop more critical reflection.
Discussion of the correlation coefficient according to Cohen is sound but omits consideration of p-value significance. Include whether correlation is statistically significant.
The chi-square analysis states âstrongly acceptingâ the null hypothesis; correct terminology is âfail to rejectâ when p>Î±.
The conclusion effectively summarises findings but repeats calculations verbatim. Instead, synthesize results concisely and highlight implications.
The reflection on survey limitations and variable selection is thoughtful and shows awareness of potential biases.
